entities.name: gandha
entities.cite: the wood's own gandha for apprehension

entities.name: saṃskāra
entities.cite: There is preparation of the sense-organ alone by concentration, collyrium and the like; but preparation of the object

entities.name: pratipatti
entities.cite: for the apprehension of its smell

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: preparation of the object for the apprehension of its smell

axioms.name: karana
axioms.cite: yogin's concentration and the doctor's eye-salve improve the eye

axioms.name: karana
axioms.cite: yogin's concentration and the doctor's eye-salve improve the eye

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: the wood's own gandha for apprehension

rejected_readings.label: indriya_preparation_only
rejected_readings.rendering: Only the sense-organ can undergo preparation; the object does not.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly establishes both modalities with the connective 'tu' (but), and the commentary treats object-preparation (crushing sandalwood) with the same samskara-vocabulary as sense-organ preparation, granting it full legitimacy within manifestation theory.

rejected_readings.label: unified_preparation_mechanism
rejected_readings.rendering: Sense-organ and object undergo preparation through the same causal mechanism and means.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary distinguishes two independent causal pathways: external means (concentration, collyrium) modify the sense-organ while leaving the object untouched; intrinsic modification (crushing sandalwood) prepares the object while leaving the sense untouched. These are structurally distinct modes, not instances of a single mechanism.

rejected_readings.label: object_preparation_is_revelation
rejected_readings.rendering: Crushing sandalwood does not truly condition the object; it merely makes its pre-existing smell more apparent.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary grants object-preparation equal weight as an empirical mode within 'a taxonomy of how hidden things come to cognition'. Treating it as mere revelation rather than true conditioning would demote the ontological status the verse and commentary accord it.

notes: Verse 1.79 instantiates Bhartrhari's manifestation phenomenology: both perceiver (indriya) and perceived (visaya) require conditioning (samskara) for cognition to arise. The dual preparation doctrine embeds sound and word within a general empirical theory of how powers become manifest, rather than treating language as ontologically sui generis. The contract brackets whether samskara represents real transformation (parinama) or apparent modification (vivarta), encoding only the shared commitment that both modes are legitimate.
